title: The Canterbury Tales. Chaucer's Tale of Meliboeus. by Geoffrey Chaucer — Love Poetry Lines
content:
THE PROLOGUE.

"No more of this, for Godde's dignity!"
Quoth oure Hoste; "for thou makest me
So weary of thy very lewedness,
That, all so wisly God my soule bless,
Mine eares ache for thy drafty speech.
Now such a rhyme the devil I beteche:
meaning: "The Canterbury Tales. Chaucer's Tale of Meliboeus." by Geoffrey Chaucer is a love and deep and nature poem consisting of 50 lines. The poet explores themes of love and deep and nature through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "THE PROLOGUE. ...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Geoffrey Chaucer's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
